whatItDoes: Generates random IPv6 Unique Local Addresses (ULA) in the fd00::/8 range. Displays each address with its /64 prefix, full expanded form, and compressed notation. Supports batch generation of up to 50 addresses with individual and bulk copy.
whyItExists: IPv6 ULAs serve the same purpose as RFC 1918 private IPv4 addresses — they provide non-routable addresses for internal networks, lab environments, and service mesh configurations. Generating them manually is tedious and error-prone.
whoShouldUse: Network engineers designing dual-stack or IPv6-only networks, DevOps engineers configuring Kubernetes pod networking, homelab enthusiasts setting up IPv6, and students learning IPv6 addressing.
useCases: Generate ULA prefixes for internal network segments during IPv6 migration planning
Create unique local addresses for Docker containers or Kubernetes pods
Set up IPv6 ULA addresses for home lab services that should not be globally routable
Generate test addresses for IPv6 firewall rules and routing configurations
instructions: Enter the number of ULA addresses to generate (1-50)
Press Generate to create the addresses
View the compressed form (with /64 prefix) for configuration files
View the full expanded form for learning or debugging
Click any address or field to copy it to your clipboard
examples: Input: Count 3 — Output: 3 unique fd00::/8 ULAs, each with random 40-bit Global IDs
bestPractices: Always use /64 prefix length for standard IPv6 subnetting — smaller prefixes break SLAAC and NDP
Store generated ULA prefixes in a central registry to avoid collisions across teams
Do not use ULAs for services that need to be reachable from the internet — use global unicast (2000::/3) instead
commonMistakes: Using fc00::/8 instead of fd00::/8 — fc00::/7 is reserved but only fd00::/8 is actually in use for ULAs
Assigning ULA addresses to internet-facing services that need global reachability
Using subnet lengths other than /64, which can break IPv6 features like SLAAC and privacy extensions
faq: What is an IPv6 ULA? — A Unique Local Address is the IPv6 equivalent of a private IPv4 address (like 192.168.x.x). It is not routable on the public internet.
Why fd00::/8 and not fc00::/8? — The fc00::/8 block was originally split into fc00::/8 (locally assigned) and fd00::/8 (random). Only fd00::/8 is in common use today.

Can two organizations have the same ULA? — Statistically extremely unlikely (40-bit random ID = ~1 trillion possibilities), but for critical infrastructure, consider registering with a coordination authority.
